spoken_input:
Safety should have been a backstop. The Commission later criticized weak problem reporting, inadequate trend analysis, misrepresented criticality, and safety structures that did not give the concern enough independent force at the decisive points.

People can care about safety while the way decisions are made still lets a warning arrive too late, too thin, or too resolved to change action.

By launch morning, the warning had passed through hardware evidence, paperwork, meetings, waivers, charts, recommendation, caucus, and management acceptance.

At the end of that route, launch was still open.

At 11:38 AM Eastern Standard Time on January 28, 1986, Challenger launched from Kennedy Space Center.

Within the first second, smoke appeared near the right aft field joint. The primary O-ring had not sealed the way it needed to seal. The secondary O-ring, affected by cold and joint movement, had not saved the joint in time.

The smoke continued through roughly T plus two and a half seconds.

Then it disappeared.

The Commission considered that combustion residue may have temporarily sealed the breach. For a short time, the vehicle could look normal while the failure chain remained alive.

That temporary quiet is one of the clearest details in the record. The physical system did what the organization had been doing for years: a sign of breach appeared, an explanation arrived, and flight continued.

Around 58 to 59 seconds into flight, flame became visible again. Hot gas from the right booster began damaging surrounding structure, including the External Tank area.

At approximately seventy-three seconds, Challenger broke apart. Francis "Dick" Scobee, Michael Smith, Judith Resnik, Ellison Onizuka, Ronald McNair, Gregory Jarvis, and Christa McAuliffe were lost.

The visible failure lasted seconds. The invisible one had been accumulating for years.

What came next was the question every institution fears: how did this happen when the evidence already existed?

President Reagan appointed the Rogers Commission, chaired by William Rogers. Richard Feynman was one of its members, and his independent work became part of the public memory of the investigation.

The physical cause was direct: failure of the seal in the right booster's aft field joint, hot gas escaping, damage to the External Tank area, and vehicle breakup.

The Commission did not stop at the physical cause.

previous_text:
as to travel. It has to move from hardware to engineers, from engineers to managers, from contractors to NASA, from lower review levels to the people with authority to stop the launch.

At each step, the signal can be compressed. Compression is normal in large programs. But when compression changes an active objection into a resolved item, the system has made a decision without naming it directly.
next_text:
It found a flawed launch decision process. It found communication failures. It found that crucial information about O-ring history, temperature concern, and continuing engineering opposition did not reach key decision-makers with the force and clarity it needed.

The people closest to the hardware had the sharpest version of the risk. The people with the authority to stop launch received a softene
